title: The Defensive Shift: Reading the Hitter

root.narration: You're the coach, and the opposing team's cleanup hitter is stepping up. This kid is a dead pull hitter -- a big, strong left-handed batter who crushes everything to the right side. In his first at-bat, he pulled a double down the right-field line. In his second, he yanked a hard grounder through the normal second base hole. Nobody's on base, nobody out, and you're up by one run. Before the pitch, you have a chance to adjust your infield positioning. How do you set up your defense?

choices.text: Shift the infield! Move the shortstop to the right side of second base, put the second baseman in shallow right field, and shade the third baseman toward short. Take away his pull side!
Stay in normal positions. He's 12 years old -- he might adjust. Don't overthink it.
Keep normal alignment but have everyone play deep. He hits the ball hard -- give ourselves more reaction time.

decision-shift-result.narration: You put the shift on. The shortstop moves to the right side of the bag, the second baseman shades into shallow right field, and the third baseman moves to the normal shortstop position. The left side of the infield is basically empty. The batter looks around, sees the shift, and steps into the box. He swings hard and pulls a sharp grounder toward the right side -- exactly where he's been hitting all day. The shifted shortstop is RIGHT THERE. He fields it cleanly. But now the batter is fuming. Next at-bat, he comes up again. Same shift?

choices.text: Keep the shift on! He's a pull hitter. He's not going to change his approach in one at-bat.
Shade back toward normal but keep a slight pull-side lean. He might try to go the other way now that he's seen the shift.
Go back to normal. He saw the shift and he'll bunt or go the other way to beat it.

outcome-keep-shift-great.narration: You keep the shift on. The batter steps in, determined to pull one through. He gets a fastball and YANKS it to the right side -- AGAIN! But the shifted shortstop is camped right there. Easy grounder, routine throw to first, and the big hitter is retired AGAIN. He slams his helmet. The shift completely neutralized his power. Two at-bats, two easy outs, when he normally would have had two hits.

outcome.headline: Shift Shuts Him Down Again! Easy Out!
outcome.explanation: Pull hitters, especially at the youth level, rarely adjust their approach mid-game. Pulling the ball is a deeply ingrained habit -- it takes weeks of practice to learn to hit the other way, not one at-bat of frustration. By keeping the shift on, you played the percentages: this hitter pulls the ball 80%+ of the time, so you put your fielders where the ball goes 80% of the time. That's not guessing -- that's scouting and strategy. In the big leagues, teams use spray charts to shift. At the youth level, you can do the same thing just by watching where the hitter hit the ball in previous at-bats.
outcome.whatToRemember: Pull hitters rarely adjust mid-game. Once you see the pattern, shift accordingly and trust the data. Put your fielders where the ball goes, not where tradition says they should stand.

outcome-adjust-good.narration: You shade back toward normal with a slight pull-side lean. Smart compromise. The batter still pulls the ball, but it's a hard grounder right up the middle. Your second baseman, who's still cheating a step toward the pull side, dives and barely snags it. He flips to first from his knees -- OUT! Close call though. If you'd kept the full shift, that ball would have been an easy play instead of a highlight-reel effort.

outcome.headline: Diving Play! Would Have Been Routine With Full Shift.
outcome.explanation: You hedged your bet, and it worked out -- barely. The slight shade toward the pull side gave you just enough to make the play, but it required a diving stop instead of a routine grounder. The full shift would have had the fielder standing right where the ball was hit. When a hitter has shown the same tendency multiple times, commit to the shift. Half-measures create difficult plays out of what should be easy ones. The only time to adjust the shift is if the hitter has shown the ability to hit the other way -- and this hitter hasn't shown that at all.
outcome.whatToRemember: Don't half-shift. Either commit to the full shift based on the hitter's tendencies, or play straight up. A half-shift creates tough plays out of what should be routine.

outcome-remove-okay.narration: You go back to normal positioning. The batter steps in and -- surprise -- he pulls it again. Hard grounder through the normal second base hole, right where it went in his first at-bat. Single to right field. Your second baseman was three steps away from where the ball went through. If you'd kept the shift on, that's a routine out. Instead, the leadoff man is on first.

outcome.headline: Single Through the Hole. Shift Would Have Had It.
outcome.explanation: You overthought it. You assumed the hitter would adjust after seeing the shift, but he didn't -- he can't help himself, he's a pull hitter. At the youth level, hitters almost never change their approach based on defensive positioning. They swing the way they swing. The fear that a hitter will 'bunt against the shift' or 'go the other way' is usually overblown, especially with power hitters who take big cuts. Trust what you've SEEN, not what you FEAR might happen. This hitter pulled the ball in every at-bat. The shift was working. Stick with what works.
outcome.whatToRemember: Trust scouting data over fear. Pull hitters pull the ball. Don't remove the shift because of what MIGHT happen when the data tells you what WILL happen.

outcome-normal-bad.narration: You keep everyone in normal positions. The batter does exactly what he's done all game -- pulls a hard grounder through the right side of the infield. It splits the gap between the first baseman and second baseman for a clean single. If you'd shifted the infield, your shortstop or second baseman would have been standing right where that ball went through. The hitter showed you his pattern three times and you didn't adjust.

outcome.headline: Single! Same Spot, Same Result!
outcome.explanation: When a hitter shows you the same tendency repeatedly, you MUST adjust. Keeping normal positions against a dead pull hitter is like leaving a door wide open and being surprised when someone walks through it. Defensive positioning isn't about where fielders 'should' stand -- it's about where the ball is GOING to go. This hitter pulled the ball to the right side in every single at-bat. That's not a coincidence; that's a pattern. Good defense is about using information. You had all the information you needed and didn't use it.
outcome.whatToRemember: Watch where hitters hit the ball and ADJUST. If they pull everything, shift. If they hit everything up the middle, shade that way. Defense is about putting fielders where the ball goes.

outcome-deep-okay.narration: You play everyone deep in normal alignment. The batter pulls a hard grounder -- your second baseman fields it cleanly at deep depth, but now the throw to first is LONG. He fires it over and the first baseman has to jump to catch it. The batter is safe by half a step! Playing deep gave you more range but cost you time on the throw. And the ball went to the pull side anyway -- a shift would have put someone right there at normal depth for an easy play.

outcome.headline: Safe! Deep Positioning Cost You Time.
outcome.explanation: Playing deep gives you more range but makes the throw to first longer, which can cost you outs on grounders. Against a pull hitter, depth isn't the solution -- lateral positioning is. The problem isn't that your fielders can't get to the ball; it's that they're standing in the wrong spots horizontally. Moving your fielders toward the PULL SIDE at normal depth would have given you the best of both worlds: the ball comes right to the fielder, and the throw to first is still a normal distance.
outcome.whatToRemember: Against pull hitters, the adjustment is lateral (side to side), not depth (in and back). Shift toward the pull side, not deeper. Depth creates long throws.
